summary: Dutch-Jewish philosopher of the 17th century, expelled from Amsterdam's Jewish community for his heterodox views. Author of Ethics (demonstrated in geometrical order) and Tractatus Theologico-Politicus. Developed pantheism ('God or Nature'), the doctrine of conatus, and substance monism. Central figure in the 1780s Pantheism Controversy involving Jacobi and Mendelssohn. Called the 'prince of philosophers' by Deleuze.

works.name: Ethics and the Geometric Method
works.description: Spinoza's masterwork Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order, is structured after Euclid's Elements with definitions, axioms, and propositions. It argues that God is the only substance, identical with Nature ('Deus sive Natura'), and possesses infinite attributes. The work contains five parts, including 'Of Human Bondage' (on the power of the affects/passions) and concludes that understanding God is the highest virtue. Its first axiom is 'Everything which exists, exists either in itself or in something else.' Roger Scruton called it 'the last indisputable Latin masterpiece.'

clues.clue: written in geometric order emulating Euclid's Elements — uses axioms, definitions, and propositions
examples: presents axioms such as 'a true idea must agree with its object' to emulate Euclid's Elements
divided up into definitions, axioms and propositions

clues.clue: 'God or Nature' (Deus sive Natura) — pantheism: God is the only substance; nature and God are the same
examples: concluded that 'God is Nature' in a 'geometrically-ordered' book
claimed that 'God, or that is Nature,' is the only substance in the universe

clues.clue: section 'Of Human Bondage' — life subject to emotions/affects means being 'under the control of fortune'
examples: describes the 'powers of the affects' in the section 'Of Human Bondage'
wrote on the passions in his chapter 'Of Human Bondage'

clues.clue: natura naturans vs. natura naturata — active productive aspect of nature (God) vs. passive aspect sustained by it
examples: contrasting 'natura naturans' with 'natura naturata'
identified God with 'natura naturans,' rather than 'natura naturata'

clues.clue: substance monism — only one substance with several facets; defined 'attribute' as 'what the intellect perceives of a substance as constituting its essence'
examples: defined 'attribute' as 'what the intellect perceives of a substance as constituting its essence'
argued that there was only one substance

clues.clue: first axiom: 'Everything which exists, exists either in itself or in something else'; 'self-caused' means 'essence involves existence'
examples: 'Everything which exists, exists either in itself or in something else' is the first axiom

examples: rejected Cartesian dualism by instead positing that there is only substance

clues.clue: ends with 'All things excellent are as difficult as they are rare'
examples: ends his most famous work by stating that 'All things excellent are as difficult as they are rare'

clues.clue: three kinds of knowledge: experientia vaga (random experience), ratio, scientia intuitiva; knowledge of the third kind comes from God
examples: divided knowledge into three increasingly clear categories: experientia vaga, ratio, and scientia intuitiva
sensory perception 'knowledge of the first kind'

clues.clue: adequate vs. inadequate ideas — active and passive states of mind; adequate ideas are rational and necessarily true
examples: related active and passive states of mind to confused 'inadequate' ideas and clear 'adequate' ones

works.name: Conatus and the Affects
works.description: The conatus principle ('each thing strives to preserve its own being') is Spinoza's theory of existential inertia, appearing as Proposition IIIP6 in the Ethics. It drives all human passions and is altered by affects like 'active joy' and 'active desire.' Joy and suffering (pleasure and pain) are feelings of increase and decrease in one's power, a formulation that sparked the 1990s 'affective turn.' Judith Butler treated conatus in 'The Desire to Live.'

clues.clue: conatus — 'each thing strives to preserve its own being'; doctrine of existential inertia driving the passions
examples: 'every man strives to preserve his own being,' a doctrine called conatus
espouses this principle, which states that each thing strives to preserve its being

clues.clue: joy and suffering as increase and decrease in power — sparked the 1990s 'affective turn'
examples: definitions of joy and suffering as the feelings of increase and decrease in our power sparked the 1990s' 'affective turn'

clues.clue: Judith Butler's 'The Desire to Live' treats conatus as a thing wanting to 'persevere in its being'
examples: Judith Butler's 'The Desire to Live' treats this philosopher's idea that a thing wants to 'persevere in its being'

works.name: Tractatus Theologico-Politicus
works.description: Published anonymously in 1670, this work argues for the separation of theology and philosophy, claims that Moses did not write the Torah (Ezra did), denies miracles as violations of natural law, and advocates religious toleration and free speech. It describes the Bible as 'imperfect, corrupt, erroneous, and inconsistent with itself,' argues the Torah was only fit as a state constitution for ancient Israel, and claims the persistence of Jews is largely due to circumcision. Steven Nadler's A Book Forged in Hell examines this work.

clues.clue: argued Moses did not write the Torah — claimed Ezra wrote it or it was a compilation; Torah only valid as ancient Israel's constitution
examples: dismisses the idea that Moses wrote the Torah
claimed that the Torah should not be followed because it was only fit to serve as the state constitution of ancient Israel

clues.clue: separation of theology and philosophy; Bible teaches 'obedience' not knowledge; argued for religious toleration and free speech
examples: argues for a clear separation between theology and philosophy
freedom of expression is important because 'those whose judgment is free and unbiased can attain success'

clues.clue: natural law disallows miracles — miracles are lawlike events whose cause we are ignorant of
examples: wrote that natural law disallows miracles
because God acts only of his own nature, miracles are lawlike events

clues.clue: persistence of Jews mainly due to circumcision; Israel was not uniquely 'chosen'
examples: the persistence of Jews as a race was mainly due to their fastidiousness in requiring circumcision
rejection of the idea that the Israelites were 'chosen'

clues.clue: Steven Nadler's A Book Forged in Hell examines this work
examples: Steven Nadler's A Book Forged in Hell examines

works.name: The Pantheism Controversy and Reception
works.description: In the 1780s, a public dispute between Friedrich Jacobi and Moses Mendelssohn over Gotthold Lessing's deathbed confession of following Spinoza became the 'Pantheism Controversy.' Jacobi accused Spinoza of 'pure materialism' and atheism, while Mendelssohn wrote Morning Hours defending theism. The controversy led to renewed interest in Spinoza and Jacobi's popularization of the term 'nihilism.' Major modern interpreters include Gilles Deleuze (who called Spinoza the 'prince of philosophers'), Antonio Negri (The Savage Anomaly), and Michael Hardt (concept of 'multitude'). Hegel interpreted Spinoza's philosophy as 'immobile.' Leo Strauss examined his 'critique of religion.'

clues.clue: Pantheism Controversy (1780s) — Jacobi vs. Mendelssohn over Lessing's Spinozism; Jacobi accused Spinoza of 'pure materialism'
examples: Moses Mendelssohn and Friedrich Jacobi got into a public dispute when Gotthold Lessing announced he was a follower of this philosopher
Jacobi accused of 'pure materialism' and thus atheism

clues.clue: Gilles Deleuze called Spinoza the 'prince of philosophers'; wrote Expressionism in Philosophy and Spinoza: Practical Philosophy
examples: Deleuze, who called him the 'prince of philosophers' and wrote Expressionism in Philosophy about him
Gilles Deleuze wrote Spinoza: Practical Philosophy

clues.clue: Antonio Negri's The Savage Anomaly; Hardt and Negri's concept of 'multitude' from Spinoza's political works
examples: The Savage Anomaly by an author who used one of his terms to title a book
Michael Hardt and Antonio Negri's Multitude takes its title concept from a work by this author

clues.clue: Leo Strauss examined Spinoza's 'critique of religion'; contrasted Spinoza (Athens/reason) with Calvin (Jerusalem/revelation)
examples: Strauss gives this philosopher as an example of Athens and John Calvin as an example of Jerusalem

clues.clue: Schelling claimed to inherit Spinoza's system; later opposed it
examples: Friedrich Schelling claimed to inherit this thinker's philosophical system

clues.clue: Hegel interpreted Spinoza's philosophy as 'immobile' (Macherey's Hegel or Spinoza)
examples: Pierre Macherey argued that later thinker misinterpreted this earlier thinker when presenting his philosophy as 'immobile'

clues.clue: stone thrown through air would think it was flying of its own free will (Schopenhauer added the stone would be right)
examples: wrote that if you threw a stone through the air, it would think it was flying of its own free will

clues.clue: Matthew Arnold described his expulsion from the Jewish community in Essays in Criticism
examples: In his Essays in Criticism, Matthew Arnold describes the expulsion of this Dutch philosopher

clues.clue: only book published under his own name was a critique of Descartes' Principles of Philosophy
examples: The only book this man published under his own name was a critique of Descartes' Principles of Philosophy

comprehensive_summary:
Baruch Spinoza (1632-1677) is one of the most frequently clued philosophers in quizbowl, with a deep and varied question set. The two universal giveaways are his pantheism ('God or Nature') and his expulsion from Amsterdam's Jewish community. His Ethics, written in geometric order emulating Euclid, is the central work.

The Ethics argues that God is the only substance, possessing infinite attributes, and is identical with Nature (Deus sive Natura). It distinguishes between natura naturans (active, productive nature/God) and natura naturata (passive nature). The work is structured with definitions, axioms, and propositions; its first axiom is 'Everything which exists, exists either in itself or in something else.' The section 'Of Human Bondage' examines how the affects (emotions) control human life, while the final section praises knowledge of the third kind (scientia intuitiva) as coming from God. Spinoza rejected Cartesian dualism, arguing mind and body are two attributes of one substance. The conatus principle ('each thing strives to preserve its own being') drives all passions and is one of the most distinctive Spinoza clues.

The Tractatus Theologico-Politicus (1670) is Spinoza's political-religious treatise arguing for separation of theology and philosophy, claiming Moses did not write the Torah (Ezra did), denying miracles as violations of natural law, and advocating free speech and religious toleration. It calls the Bible 'imperfect, corrupt, erroneous, and inconsistent with itself' and argues the Torah was only valid as ancient Israel's state constitution.

The 1780s Pantheism Controversy, where Friedrich Jacobi and Moses Mendelssohn disputed over Lessing's Spinozism, is a major reception clue. Jacobi accused Spinoza of materialism and atheism; Mendelssohn wrote Morning Hours in response. Modern reception includes Gilles Deleuze (who called Spinoza the 'prince of philosophers'), Antonio Negri (The Savage Anomaly, concept of 'multitude'), Leo Strauss (Spinoza's 'critique of religion'), and Mark Fisher (K-Punk blog on Spinoza and cybernetics). Spinoza's letters, signed with a rose above the Latin word for 'cautiously,' addressed topics including the infinite (to Lewis Meyer) and the problem of evil (to Willem van Blijenbergh).

cards.front: Work: structured after Euclid's Elements with axioms, definitions, and propositions; argues God is the only substance identical with Nature ('Deus sive Natura')
Concept: the distinction between the active, productive aspect of nature identical to God, and the passive aspect sustained by it — two Latin terms for 'nature naturing' and 'nature natured'
Concept: 'each thing strives to preserve its own being' — the doctrine of existential inertia that drives all human passions, appearing as Proposition IIIP6
Work: section 'Of Human Bondage' examines how the affects control human life; final section praises knowledge of the third kind as coming from God
Work: argues Moses did not write the Torah, denies miracles, and advocates religious toleration and free speech; Steven Nadler called it 'A Book Forged in Hell'
Philosopher: the 1780s Pantheism Controversy between Jacobi and Mendelssohn erupted over Lessing's deathbed confession of following this thinker

Philosopher: Gilles Deleuze called this thinker the 'prince of philosophers' and wrote Expressionism in Philosophy about him; placed between Duns Scotus and Nietzsche as theorizers of the univocity of being
Philosopher: Antonio Negri's The Savage Anomaly and Hardt and Negri's Multitude draw on this thinker's political concept of the 'multitude'

Philosopher: argued that two rational men can form a composite being twice as powerful as either; equated 'right' with 'power' and argued the sovereign has limited power since everyone is 'the master of his own thoughts'
Philosopher: the only book published under this thinker's own name was a critique of Descartes' Principles of Philosophy, done 'in geometrical order'
